title: Yorkshire curd tart
subtitle: The old Yorkshire cheese and currant tart, scented with nutmeg
excerpt: A filling of fresh curd cheese, currants, butter, and egg, lightly spiced with nutmeg and baked in a shortcrust case. A regional British tart with roots in the dairy farming of the Yorkshire Dales.
sourceNotes: Yorkshire curd tart is documented in Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), Project Gutenberg, as a regional specialty of the Yorkshire Dales connected to the sheep-farming and dairy calendar. The curd filling made from fresh cheese, currants, and nutmeg is attested in Yorkshire cookery from the eighteenth century and remains a specialty of Dales farmers' markets and tearooms.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day; refrigerate and eat within two days.

glossaryTerms.definition: Fresh, unaged cheese made by separating curds from whey. Mild in flavour and slightly grainy in texture; similar to cottage cheese but drained and pressed firmer. Ricotta or drained cottage cheese can substitute.

content.text: Yorkshire curd tart is one of the old regional British pastries that most people outside Yorkshire have never tried. The filling is made from

, the fresh, mild dairy product made by separating milk curds from whey, mixed with butter, currants, egg, and sugar, then generously spiced with nutmeg. The result is a filling that is light, slightly grainy, and fragrant; less sweet than a cheesecake and much less rich.

True curd cheese from a farm dairy gives the best flavour, but drained cottage cheese or ricotta produces a very similar result at home. The pastry case is not blind baked; the filling is placed in the raw case and everything bakes together, which gives a softer base — this is traditional.

attrs.prepNote: cold, for the pastry; plus 50 g softened for the filling
freshly grated; or about 1 tsp pre-ground

content.text: Rub the pastry-quantity cold {{unsalted-butter}} into {{plain-flour}} and the pastry {{caster-sugar}} until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Add the {{egg-yolks}} and 2 tablespoons of cold water; mix to a firm dough. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Roll out and line a 23 cm loose-bottomed tart tin. Refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan (200°C conventional). Beat the filling-quantity softened {{unsalted-butter}} with the filling {{caster-sugar}} until combined. Beat in {{eggs}} one at a time. Stir in {{cream-cheese}}, {{currants}}, {{nutmeg-whole}}, and the zest from {{lemon}}. Pour the filling into the uncooked pastry case. Bake for 35–40 minutes until the filling is set and lightly coloured on top, and the pastry is golden. The centre should have a very slight wobble. Cool in the tin for 30 minutes before slicing.
The pastry base will be softer than a blind-baked case; this is correct for the traditional version. For a crisper base, blind bake for 10 minutes before adding the filling.
Freshly grated nutmeg is markedly more fragrant than pre-ground. The quantity here is generous; reduce to half a teaspoon if you prefer a more subtle spice.
The tart is traditionally eaten at room temperature, not cold from the fridge.
